The Way They Do in Our Sister State to
Rob the People.
The following written The Gloue by
Mr. Frank Atkinson, a newspaper man
of Chicago, is worth looking after. It
should be known that North Carolina
people are not robbing men : Atkinson
says :
"Mr. James A. Wenoby, of Philadelphia,
is at the Palmer House with a wild tale
of woe. According to his statement Mr.
Wenoby is $G0,000 worse off now than
he was some few months ago when he
went down into South Carolina to invest
some of his spare change. It came about
in this way :
44 'I had heard,' said he, 'of the excel
lent opportunities for investment in
South Carolina and determined to go
down and investigate. I was treated
royally. There were banquets and drives,
fox hunts and everything to make life
pleasant for me. The men I met en
larged on the magnificent resources of
the state, gave satisfactory explanations
as to why it hadn't been developed be
fore, the war, lack of capital, etc.
"'What we need,' said my friend, 'is
northern capital. We've got the re
sources, any God's- quantity of them. All
we want is the capital.'
"'When my enthusiasm has been
worked to the proper pitch four or five
members of the Horseshoe & Swepson
company offered to let me in on a deal in
Cherokee county and I decided to rmy
$00,000 for 20,000 acres I got abstracts
of the property and everything was ap
parently all straight. Shortly after the
money had been paid over, however, and
I began to make some improvements I
received notice from several individuals
to the effect that they had bought that
same property direct from the state for
twenty cents per ncre and they exhibited
deeds with the state seal upon them. I
afterward found that it was the custom
to issue patents to the same property
over and over in this way and that thev
call this species of robbery slip-shucking.'
Such was the story of Mr. James A.
Menoby, of Philadelphia."

THE FIREMAN AND CREW ALL DEAD.
A Wreck on the Western North Carolina
Road Near Statcsville This
Morning.
Meagre reports have reached Durham
of a wreck on the Western North Caro
lina road this morning.
As to what really happened The Globe
cannot tell. Engineer Hicks, of a pas
senger train here at noon, said that there
had been a wreck and that Billy West,
the engineer, was killed. He also said
that the fireman and all the crew, save
the conductor, had met their death.
It was passenger train and another re
port says all were killed.
The hour was so late that at this writ
ing it has been impossible to get any fur
ther information or anything definite.
A wild rumor floated just as we went
to press to the effect that the Asheville
fire boys had been killed, but no credit is
attached to the rumor.
We have tried all ways possible to get
further information but have failed to
this time.
Another report says, and it is ap
parentljr authentic, as it came by wray of
Raleigh, that forty persons were killed.
There were ten of the Asheville fire com
pany who left here last night. It is sup
posed that they all were killed.
The accident happened three miles the
other side of Statesville. The train went
through a bridge, which high waters had
washed out.

TO RAISE SUMATRA HERE.
Planters Forced to Come to This Country
by the McKinley Kill.
Chicago, Aug. 25. (Special to The
Globe.) If Mr. J. Kaufmann of Deli
Sumatra is not mistaken the United
States wiU be raising its own Sumatra
tobacco before very long.
"I am now on my way south," said he
at the Auditorium, "to look into the sur
roundings there. If the plan proves
feasible plantations for raising Sumatra
tobacco will be established there. I have
uo doubt that a leaf as good for practical
purposes as that raised in the Island of
Sumatra can be shown in this country.
i It may not be quiet so long as the natiue
leas, but this will make but little differ
ence in its commercial value.
""We have been compelled to take some
action of this sort," continued Mr. Kauf
mann, "by the McKinley bill. Since its
passage forty of the largest tobacco
plantations in Sumatra have been closed,
entailing an aggregate loss to the owners
of $20,000,000. In anticipation of the
rise in Sumatra all the big dealers in the
United States laid in a large supply and
the consequence is the market is glutted."
Speaking of the system under "which
the plant is cultivated in Sumatra, Mr.
Kaufmann said: "The plantations are
leased from the native Malay chieftains
for a period of 75 years. The Malays
still control the interior of the country
which is rich in minerals, but the Malays
prefer to live in their own way and are
hostile to any new enterprise, so that
little or no attempt has been made to
develop these enterprises. The labor on
the plantations is dene by coolies, 15,000
of whom have been thrown out of
employment. They are hired for a period
of three year and during that period
the authority of the managers of the
plantations over them is almost abso
lute. They are furnished with dwellings
and what little clothing they need. They
are also given the tobacco seed and each
assigned a piece of ground. The first
part of the product is taken to pay for
the food, clothing, etc., advanced ; then
a certain proportion of the balance be
longs to the coolie. The year's income
of a coolie after these necessary expenses
are paid ranges from $30 to $g0. Some
mcke as low as $5 and some are even in
debt at the end of three years' service.
These are obliged to remain and work
until this debt is paid. If they attempt
to run away they are arrested by order
of the 4)urch governor, brought back
and delivered over to the manager of the
plantation for punishment. A coolie
usually gets about 25 lashes for the first
offense and is not able to walk for a fort
night. It really makes little difference
whether a coolie has $5 or $00 profit to
show at the end of the year, as it is all
gambled away as soon as he gets it."
F. A.

IN the reception rom of Dr. Roger
Campbell, surgeon to the English
Hospital at King Po, there hang
many scrolls and tablets which
have been presented to him by grateful
patients whom he has relieved of
Ickness and disease. The most highly
prized of them all is the one which
hangs directly over the mantel and
hears the Inscription In golden characters:
He clvea his skill and wledom
'J'o ?ave the llvea of men.
' .It Is a painting on silk, by one of
the famous old artists of the early
days, and consists of a Chinese landscape,
with a venerable white-bearded
figure In the foreground and a waterfall
and cloudy mountains In the back.
Just how It came to be presented to
the doctor and just how his skill once
aved a human life is one of the most
furious and interesting of the many
tzperiences he has had in his eastern
fife.
The Hongkong Dally Press of a
date not so many years ago, contained
the following Item: "Regrettable In*
Olden t In the Colony."
"We are sorry to have to record the
killing of a British seaman by some
of the mourners In a funeral party
yesterday. All the particulars are not
Pet at hand, but, as far as can be ascertained
at present, It seems that
Hugh McLean, an able-bodied seaman
pf H. M. collier Ben Lo/nond, attempted
to cross the funeral procession of
Our esteemed citizen Mr. Tak Hoy
(late compradore of the Nlng 1*0 Rice
Company), and, stumbling against the
carriers of one of the banners, was
knocked down and beaten or choked
death before he'could be rescued.
"The jlnrlksha coolie who was pulling
him escaped and gave the alarm,
and all the parties concerned were
apprehended by the police, who are
giving the case their careful investigation.
Such unfortunate occurrences
were not unknown in the early days
At ?Vin iirklin tv.n.,',, KAol^annn keen
V* mo v? iiuu mail a icoiucuto iiciu,
but it has been many years since anything:
so serious has happened to disturb
our usual friendly relations with
pur Chinese citizens. We trust that
pn Investigation It will bo found that
there was some misapprehension on
the part of the funeral party and that
there was really no Intention on their
part of Inflicting fatal Injuries."
"Well, Brownlow," said Inspector
Wallace to his assistant, the following
evening, as they were going over
the caste at headquarters, "what have
tvo got so far? You see. It's a little
different from a merchant vessel; this
Was a naval collier and so the officers
and sailors on the station are all
rtlrred up about it and swear they'll
ave justice done and all that sort of
thing."
"Yes, old Collins, the skipper, stopped
mp on Queen's road this morning
nd said it was one of the most brutal
fnurders he had ever heard of, and.
n his opinion, they ought to hang
the whole 'funeral gang,' as he called
them."
"What did you get out of the rlksha
pian? I suppose, as usual, he was so
cared he wouldn't say a word?"
"No, sir, he calmed down all right
after we assured him they weren't going
to harm him and gave us the story
U straight. It was just this way;
You see, they were marching along
With gongs and firecrackers and all
that sort of thing, and about twenty
foolles were carrying the big 'long
lfe box' with old Tak Hoy in It, when
they reached the cross-road down beyond
the Happy Valley. McLean had
Blred the coolie 'to pull him all over
the island on a hind of spree and he
turned up there Just as the chaps
currying the silk banner In front of
the coffin got there; and, as he cried,
Get across quick, John,' the coolie
tried to pass in front of the bo* and
Of course they bumped into each
Other and then the trouble began,
the riksha upset and the banner fell
down and then the procession all
topped and they piled on top . of the
Oallor and beat him and yelled all
arts of curses at him?you know how
superstitious they are about crossing
a funeral?and tlieri, not content
With that, they rammed pand down
his throat and choked him to death,
flow that's the plain truth of It, sir. I
don't suppose they really Intended to
kill him, but w"hen they get wild and
Oxclted like that they're as bad as
{he Malays running amuck, and they
don't stop to think of the consequences."
"Can you find out exactly who did
the choking?"
"You see, sir, we've questioned closely
the box-bearers and they all agree
?"-and this Is the strangest part of it
all, sir?that It was old Tak Hoy's
own son. It seems he's a kind of halfwitted
chap, that's only been down
topre from the Nlng Po country a
ahort time."
"I suppose he felt It his duty to
avenge the Insult to his dead father,
didn't he? That seems to be the Chi
peso Idea."
"That's what they all think, sir."
"Have the natives been able to get
Jllm yet?"
^"They're out after him now, sir. It
eems the old women of the family
trill have him surely before morning."
i "See that he's closely locked up
Jrhen they bring him In, and don't
let any of his family or friends see
Aim on any account."
"Right you are, sir," answered the
{aithful Brownlow.

Predicting a terrible war with Japan

gration clause be eliminated from
the proposed Japanese-American con
vention, Senator J. II. Sanford of

"If the coolie clause in not allowed

and thousands of coolies will enter
the country and without an anti
alien law to hold them In check, will
fill every part of tho rich valleys of
California.
"Tho Japanese not only become
farmers and laborers, but they also
become lessees of lands and the coin
merce of California would soon be
under the domination of a race that
cannot co-mlugle with Americans.
"The best way to handle the ques
tion is to meet It firmly like Amer
ican patriots; to have America for
Americans. This is a white man's
country, and the white man must run
It."

r %
Warships Reach Dublin for
Carrying Prisoners to
England.
c k
Dublin, Jan. 51.?More than 000 per
sons were arrested in a countrywide
search of rtffds by the military last
night and today, it is estimated. The
raids werc^made under the direction"
of the Dublin Castle authorities*, and ?
were conducted with the greatest se- ,
crecy. not even the police being ad- 1
vised of the plans in advance. Not- j
withstanding the secrecy, it is under-j
stood that only twenty of the fifty
members of the Dublin corporation
council marked for arrest have been
captured.
Newly elected Sinn Fein town offi
cials were rounded up in various parts
of Ireland. The raid, which was not
entirely unexpected, is taken as the
government's answer to the defiance
of the Sinn Fein organization signi
fled by the appointment of independ
ent officials.
Warships arrived in Dublin Bay to
nixht for the purpose, it Is uderstood.
of carrying the prisoners across the ;
Irish Sea on their way to Wormwood |
Scrubbs prison. The prisoners were
brought into Dublin today on special j
trains.
There is talk of a general strike in I
retaliation for the wholesale arrests j
The labor men have decided to make j
?ommon cause with the Sinn Feiners. !

ITALY'S PRESS BITTER
ON JUGO-SLAV REPLY
Homo. Jan. 31.?"The Jugo-Slavs'
answer to the allied propositions for
settlement of the Adriatic dispute 1s
absolutely unsatisfactory." the Idea
Xazionale declares. "The answer
is not worthy o/ serious considera
tion," then ewspaper added.
The entire press voiced Indigna
tion at the Jugo-Slavic refusal.
In the opinion of the Tribuna.
"the reply of the Belgrade govern
ment is worthy of the best Balkanic
traditions" and in fact "exceeds any
previous Balkan diplomatic prod
uct"
The scope of the answer is to an
nul the ultimatum, the Idea Nazion
ale continued, declaring Jugo-Slavla
would rediscusa anew the whole
Adriatic question, "dragging Italian
dignity into the mire."
"The Italian government has a pre
cise duty," this newspaper added.
"It would be a farce to resume the
stand taken on the 2Qth or to recog
nise the validity of payments.**
Premier Nitti. according to the
Giornale P'Italia, "would assume a
tremendous responsibility in re
reding from the atitude he has
taken. %
'Belgrade is taging a great anti
Italian demonstration." the newspa
per said, "but the nation who rose
acainst Germany and crushed Aus
tria cannot bo influenced by the
crass mob of Trumbltch bravados.'
The Tempo's Trieste correspond
ent has telgraphed that great na
tionalist demonstrations occurred at
Belgrade, I^aibach and Agram, urg
ing the government to resist the
allied ultlmaum.
The Jugo-Slavic press, according
to the correspondent, launched vio
lent attacks upon Italy.

?The government announced to lay
it had accepted "in principle" the
proposition of an American firm to
buy twenty-six former Gorman ships
seized during the war for $35,000,000.
The official statoraent pointed out
that the government in power when
the ships were seized did not make
Brazilian possession outright. For
that reason, the note said, the gov
ernment would be obligated to in
demnify Germany for use and re
tention of the vessels.
The government's announcement
followed violent protests In the
press when reports of the sale were
published here. Anti-American prop
agandists took advantage of the
opportunity to declare the American
peace delegates at the Paris con
ference backed up Brazil so they
could "grab stolen property.'*
Later Medieros Albuquerque, editor
of Afohla and known for his anti
American sentiments, flled a violent
ly worded protest in the Federal
court.
New York. Jan. 31.?The Ameri
can Ship and Commerce Corpora
tion. of New York. mentioned
ine arlier Rio de Janeiro dispatches
as the Arm back of the purchase
of the former German ships, today
said aerents of their company had
not completed their negotiations.

Judge McMahon in the Police
Court yesterday held Martin N*. Jef
ferson for action of the grand jury
in $5,000 bonds, and fixed February
10 as the time for a preliminary
hearing. Jpfferson is charged with '
forgery. He was arrested at Camp
Humphreys, where he was serving
as a soldier.
It is alleged the young man, im
personating another person, at
tempted to pass bogus checks on
tho Federal National Bank. It is j
said Jefferson confessed to W. W.
Wallace, special agent of the De- I
partment of Justice, that he wrote
the bogus check which he attemptfd
to pass on the Washington bank.
He also will be charged with pars
ing a worthless check for $100 on
officers at Camp Humphreys, signed
"Fred Jones."

the town by a thousand, more or lass,

has been cheated.
"For this there is only one remedy
take the coin he offers to return,
insert it in your pocket, face nor'
nor" west, place him immediately in
front of you facing in the same direc
tion, and then gently but firmly de
scribe an arc of a circle with the
right foot, momentum of said foot be
ing in the neighborhood of nine
knots, so that its course of swing will
be interrupted by the region of his
hip pocket.
"A bas le 'ricksha coolie! He is a
useful method of transportation, but
for being cunning he has it all over
the taxi driver of our own land!"

CHAPTER XXXIII.
A man stood In ono of the windows,
bis flguro conspicuous against the
night In cool white linen of a semi
military cut, his extended right hand
training a revolver on tho Irishman's
bead.
"Faith!" cried O'Rourko with genu
Ino relief, "you're more welcome than
a snowfall In Hades. Good evening to
ye, and many of them."
"Hands up!"
"With all tho pleasure in tho world."
O'Rourke elevated his hands. "I've
two revolvers on mo person," he vol
unteered amiably; "before ye go any
further ye'll be wanting to take 'em
away from me, I'm not doubting."
"From what I see, I quite bellevo 1
shall," agreed the Englishman, with
out relaxing his unprejudiced attitude.
"At all events, keep your hands wher
they are, for the time being. . . .
What the deuce does this mean?"
"Tell me yourself and I'll make y
a handsome present," returned the
O'Rourke .Composedly. "I've been ad
dllng me' wits over it for the last
thirty minutes, but neither rhyme not.
reason can I read Into it. Out, e
now: would ye mind relieving me ol
the arsenal I've been telling ye about
that I may rest me arms without feai
of being punctured?"
The other laughed shortly and en
tored the room a clean-limbed
sturdy, well set-up boy of four or five-and-twenty,
or thereabouts. He pos
sessed, aside from an emphatic and
capable manner, good looks enhanced
by a wide good-humored mouth.
"You might .help me out a bit, you
know," sold the boy briskly. "You'v
been so free with your information
that I don't doubt you will place me
still further under obligation to yon
by turning your back and depositing
your weapons on that tabic Of course,
I needn't bore you by remarks upon
the folly of fnlso moves."
" 'Twould bo quite superfluous," to
plied O'Rourke, obeying with a fall
and ensy grace. "There now. What
else may ho your pleasure?"
"Move back three paces and stand
still."
"RIght-O, me lord."
O'Rourke executed tho prescribed
evolution and, at rust, heard footsteps
behind him; a thought Inter ho lelt
the Englishman's hands rapidly going
- ' - ii '
A Man Stood In One of th'o Windows.
through his pockets. Then, with a
"vory good," the latter stepped be
tween tho tablo and O'Rourko and
faced him.
"You'vo apparently told tho truth
thus far," ho said. "Now whnt'dyou
know nbout this?" He waved a hand
round the room. "Re careful what
you say. I may as well Inform you
I'm Couch, lieutenant sub-chief of
pollco for this district"
"Saint Patrick would bo no moro
welcotno," declared O'Rourko. "I was
on the point of trying to get ye by
telephone when ye saved mo tbo trou
ble. How the dlvvla did yo happen
to drop In so opportunely?"
"I was coming up-stream In tho po
llco launch, on the night tour of In
spection, and stopped at the landing
Just below this Uio grounds hero run
down to UKTrlrer, you know to tele
phone back to headquarters on busi
ness. Tho oxchango operator suggest
ed I look In here and see if everything
was all right said ho'd been unable
to get any response since nightfall. .
. . Now?"
Carefully and conclsoly O'Rourke
wove tho events of tho day into a
straight narrative, starting with tho
dollvory to Sypher of tho Pool of
Flamo, touching briefly upon Dea Tro
bes' part so far as ho understood it
and concluding with the death of
the coolie. Tho sub-chief of. police
eyed him throughout with gravoly
concentrated interest, nodding his
understanding.
"I sco," he said slowly, "You make
It clear enough. Moreover, you've
convinced me. I didn't really bellevo
from tho first youi hftd any hand in
this ghastly mess, but I couldn't tnko
chances, of course. You're at liberty
to take up these pistols as soon ns you
please; In fact, I advise you to do
bo Immediately. From what's taken
placo already, you may have need of
'em within the next ten seconds. . .
. Now for this coollo. If he's nblo to
speak, I'll get somo information out of
him."
"'TIs too far gone he is, I'm fear
ing." "We'll soon find out." The English
man bent over the man, who was now
vory quiet, but, by the constnnt flick
er of his cunning eyes, still conscious,
A hasty examination told the Investi
gator all he needed to know about the
naturo of the wound. "He'll not last
long," said Lieutenant Couch, nnd bo
gan to converse with tho local ver
nacular of Pldgln-Engltsh, nbout ono
word in ten of which w.-ib intclllglbto
to O'Rourke. As he continued to speak
the coolie's scowl darkened nnd he In
torcupted with n negative motion of
his hend. Tho sub-chief repented his
rcmarkB with emphasis. For reply ho
got a monosyllable that sounded, as
much ns anything" else, like an oath.
Couch looked up. "Ho says ho wants
wntor, and I suspect he won't speak
until he gets It. Can you?"
O'Rourko fetched tho half-empty
carafe nnd Couch put It to tho coolie's
lips, permitting him to drink ns much
as ho lilted. Rut as soon as the bottlo
was removed tho fellow shut his
mouth like n trap and refused a word
In answer to the lieutenant's demands
and persuasions.
"Stubborn brute," growled Couch.
"Most of these animals hero belong to
some devllsh tong or other, and they'd
rather die than say anything touching
on the business of the society or af
fecting the interests of a brother
member. But I think I know a way to
bring him to reason. Hand me that
knife, .plenso."
Wondering, O'Rourke tendered him
the weapon that had brought death tp
Sypher. Tho lieutenant wiped It cal
lously on a corner of the coollo's
blouse and held the keen shining
blade before his eyes, accompanying
the action with a few emphatic
phrases. A curious expression, com
pounded of sullen fury and abject pan
ic fright, showed In the Chinaman's
eyes, nnd his lips were as If by magic
unsealed. However reluctant, he be
gan to chatter and spoke at length,
delivering himself of a long state
ment which Couch punctured now nnd
again with pertinent, lending ques
tions. At length, throwing aside the knlfo,
ho jumped up, strong excltemont burn
ing In hts eyes. "I've go: enough from
him," he said rapidly. "I'll explain
later. You'll help of course; your
wife's Involved as well ob Miss Pyn
sent. Rut I don't think you need fear;
wo'll be In tlmo. Are you ready? .
. . Half a minute; I've got to use
that telephone." t
Ho ran out Into tho ball, rang up
nnd shouted a number Into tho re
ceiver, and for a few moments spoke
rnpldly in a nurmese dialect.
O'Rourko gathered that he was speak
ing With a native subordinate at tho
pollco headquarters In Rangoon.
Couch swung' back Into tho study.
"Got those revolvers, sir? Then come
along; wo'll havo to run for 1L For
tunately our launch Is handy; other
wise . . ."
Ho sprang across tho veranda nnd
down to tho lawn, O'Rourko pelting
after him.
CHAPTER XXXIV.
A night of volvct blackness, softly
opaquo, lay upon land nnd wnter. Tho
police launch, shuddering with tho vi
brations of a motor running at high
tension, sped down tho silent renchea
of Rangoon River llko a hunted ghost.
She ran without lights, these having
been extinguished by Couch's direc
tions, regardless of harbor regulations
or danger. Happily tbo hour was late
enough to relievo them of much fear
of trouble with other craft; tho upper
reaches of tho river were practically
deserted.
In tho bow Couch was handling tho
wheel with tho nonchalanco of ono
from whom tho river had no secrets
by night or day. To O'Rourko It scorned
no light task to pilot so slight a craft
ut such high speed through that
Stygian darkness; yet tho sub-chler
was accomplishing tho feat without a
dlsccrnable traco of fear or tremor
of uncertainty.
O'Rourke sat beside him. In tho
stern a police orderly acted as me
chanic, attending to tho motor. Thesu
three, no moro, made up tbo rescue
party.
Though devoured by lmpatlcnco and
anxiety, O'Rourke forbore to question
Couch, hesltntlng to divert his atten
tion from his task and knowing that
bb soon as ho could tho young lieu
tenant would speak. From tho time
when the coollo bad yielded, there
bad been not a second's rest for eith
er; neither had had tlmo to confer
save on quostluus of the most lmnio
dtato moment; and control of these
Couch bad voluntarily and naturally
assumed, deciding, acting and direct
ing In the same thought, apparently.
"Your wife, with Miss PynsenL"
said Couch abruptly, without looking
round "at least I presume It's Mrs.
O'Rourke, from what you say havo
been kidnaped by a gang of highbind
ers and are now aboard a Junk In tho
1 lower river, which will sail for God
- knows-where at the turn of the tldo.
That's the only thing that saves 'em.
We'll be on 'em before thoy're able to
forco a wny down the river."
O'Rourko groaned, holding hts head
with both hands. "My wlfo . . .1"
be said brokenly,
1 "I know," Couch Interrupted grim-
1 ly; "I know how you feel. Miss Pyn-
i
sent is there, too, you sec."
"Oh," said O'Rourke, "I dldn'.t un
derstand that . . . I'm sorry." Ho
dropped a hand on the younger man's
shoulder and let It rest tboro briefly.
"Please God," he said rovcrcntly.
"there'll bo many another polluted
yellow soul yammering nt tho gates
ot hell this night!"
"Amen!" said Couch. . . .We
sha'n't bo long now."
Silently O'Rourke removed his cont
and waist-coat, hts collar and lawn
tie, and turned back his cuffs. "Even
ing clothes are hardly the thing to
fight In," he said; "but I'm thinking
'twon't mako a deal of difference to
me. Got nny cartridges for a Wobloy
mark IV?"
"Wheeler has. Give Colonel
O'Rourke a few, Wheeler," said Couch,
addressing the orderly.
Tho latter rummnged In a locker
and pressed Into O'Rourke's hand halt
a dozen cartridges, with which tho
adventurer proceeded to replenish tho
empty chamber In his rovolver.
"I'd only discharged ono," ho ob
served, "but 'tis likely we'll need that
even, with only tho thrco ot us
against a Junk-lond."
"Oh, I telephoned for reinforce
ments, of course," returned Couch.
"They ought to bo thero ahead ot
us."
"What did tho coolie tell ye, If yo've
tlmo to talk?"
Couch laughed. "I daresay you're
wondering how I made him speak at
all."
"That's the true word for ye."
"I threatened to cut off his Billy pig
tall and send him naked and dishonor
ed to the ghostly halls of bis ances
tors. It's wonderful how much those
callous brutes dote on that decora
tion. I told him further, that If he
lied, when I found it out I'd return
and shave him bald as an egg, even if
he were dead by that time. So I per
suaded the truth from him, the whole
story from his side of It."
"I'm listening. . . "
"He confessed he was In the pay
like these chaps we're after now of
a highly respectable Chinese merchant
nnd head of ono of tho tongs ono of
the richest men In Rangoon, who, It
seems, was also after thut ruby. I
enn't Imagine what he wanted of It,
but that'll come out, probably; the
man's rich enough to buy dozens ot
stones as fine. However ... I
gather ho'd iald hts plan far abend.
The coolies Intimated you'd been
watched all the way from Bombay. At
all events, tho brutes were ready
whon you arrived; Sypher was a
doomed man from tho moment )ou
handed over the Pool of Flamo. They
surrounded his bouse this night, com
ing up from the river, Just ns Boon as
It was dark enough to conceal their
actions. Then they found n third ele
ment In tho business your friend Des
Trebes, nil unsuspicious of them, lurk
ing on the vernr.da and watching Sy
pher through tho window. So they
waited to see what he was up to. And
pretty soon thoy found out. Sypher
camo downstairs, went to tho safo nni
opened It; I presume ho had the stone
In his hand, ready to put away. Whllo
ho was standing thero the Frenchmnn
slipped up behind nnd stabbed
him, annexing tho Btonc and leav
ing the way ho got In. The In
stant ho stepped off tho vernndn tho
Chlncso got him; but he managed to
scream beforo they could silence him
and drew tho attention of the house
hold, Miss Pynsent, your wife and the
servants. So to cover things up they
bad to gather them all In. The serv
ants were killed there were three ot
them and tho women . . ."
Neither man spoko for a time. Then
Couch resumed.
"This coollo wns an outsider a
servant of tho merchant's not ono
pt the Junk gang; bo ho stayed ashore,
and thought It would be a fine young
scheme to return nnd do a little loot
ing on his own . . , I've telephon
ed tbo head 0(11 co to nrrcst that
cursed merchant and contlscato bis
houso nnd goods nnd detain anybody
they could catch connected with him.
Tho net's well enough laid, nnd I
think . . ."
Tho lights of the city became vis
ible, strung along the right bank ot
tho river as the launch rounded a
bond. Couch swung tho little boat
out Into midstream. "Half-speed,
Wheeler," he said, nddlug to O'Rourko:
"I've got to pick out that Junk. I pre
sume tho right one will have all sail
Bet and be moving downstream with
tbo tide; it's Just on the turn now
nnd fortunately there's no wind worth
mentioning. ... I wish I could
see something ot the othor launch."
Ho peered anxiously Into tho obscur
ity nhead. "If there were only star
lightI" he complained bitterly.
"Stand by, Wheeler, to stop tho motor.
Wo'll drop nlong6ldo with tho current,
as quietly as we can. Colonel
O uourke, will you got forward and
talto tho boatbook and headWTrp,
ji -ho; I'm needed at thn wlni .nd
Wheeler at tho cnglno u'ltil vc make
fast"
(To bo Continued.)
Faithful to Duty.
When the army of Pompey stormed
and took Jerusalem, at the moment
tho tcmplo was taken, the priests
wero engaged with tho dally sacrifice,
and amid all the horrors which sur
rounded them, thoy continued their
tolemn duties unmoved, thinking It
better to suffer whatever came upon
them at th,elr very altars thnn to omit
an thing their law required

The Temple
Gong
.
'T Y ELL, Brownlow," said In
% /\ I spector Oubblris of the
y V Shanghai police to his
chief assistant, "what do
you make it out to be, an accident or
a regular old case of incendiarism'.'"
?"Looks rather queer for an accident
to start out In about four different di
rections at the same time, doesn't It?"
replied the captain, as he proceeded to
take off his rubber coat and cap cover
?>id brush the cinders and dust off
them, "and they can't work oft the
old-fashioned excuse about ,'carelcss
" DIM' this time, either. I know these
<haps are awfully careless about fire,
but even Chinese carelessness doesn't
burst out In four distinct spots at ex
actly the same time, does It, now?"
'"No, that's true. There might have
been one or two careless coolies
amoklng In the godown (warehouse)
?when the watchman wasn't look
ing: "
"Or Just as likely," interrupted the
captain, "the watchman himself when
the English quartermaster wasn't
looking "
?"Yes, but hardly four of them In
four different parts of the building,
eh?"
"Hardly, No, sir, you can be sure
that thing was all carefully planned
out beforehand and those four fires
Malted by some kind of slow match
concealed In the cotton and arranged
?o as to get a good old headway on
before we could get an alarm turned
In. It's Incendiarism pure, and simple',
tnat's what it Is, sir, und now I sup
pose It's up to us and the department,
as usual, to find out who the fire
brand was." ? '
The conflagration to which our two
officers were referring had taken
place about 11 o'clock In the evening
In the old godown of MacMillan
Co., near the lower Australian wharf,
and before the alarm had been given
and the foreign engines and firemen
had been able to respond thousands
of taels' worth of valuable goods had
been destroyed. The curious circum
stance, as the captain said, was that
It seemed to break out in four differ
ent places at almost the same moment
of time. The first alarm was given
by the native watchman at the go
down of the Australian mail, adjoin
ing MacMinan's on the north; the sec
ond by a Jin-rlk-sha coolie, who was
passing up the street on the south
and saw smoke curling out from
under (he eaves; the (third by two old
. women who were having their g09d
ntght tea on the second-story veranda
of the house across tlje way on the
west, and the fourth by the deck
watch of the Nlngo-Po steamer lying
In the stream almost directly east.
When the police' and firemen arrived
Ihey found the Iron doors and Bhut
ters to the windows all intact and
apparently securely locked, so that
they were abliged to break a passage
In for the hose with axes, and this It
was that made them conclude at once
that the fire had been started some
hours before and must have been
actually smoldering when the go
down was closed and fastened up for
the night at sunset, Tt was no vei^y
atrange occurrence for a fire caused
b? carelessness to smolder on for
several hours and finally burst out
Into flame later In the night; Indeed,
the Chinese dropping of hot aslies
from their pipes in between the cot
ton bales generally produced this re
milt; but for four different fires to
burst out simultaneously in four dif
ferent parts of a building looked to
every one as If something far more se
rious than carelessness had been at
work, and the entire foreign com
munity on reading the account In the
papers next morning agreed at once
with Capt. Brownlow in his conclu
sion.
The agents of the English and
French insurance companies, with a
promptness and courtesy character
istic of the far east, at once set their
appraisers and adjusters to work, and
in a very few days Messrs. MacMillan
* Co. received from them checks to
cover their loss in full, and arrange
ments were made to rebuild the
burned-out godown. So far so good,
but Inspector Gubbins had not yet
been able to report to the British con
sul that either he or any member of
hfs force had laid hands upon the in
cendiary, nor had they eben able to
solve the mysterious question as to
what purpose he had in committing
the deed. The Chinese detectives on
th? force very naturally concluded
that it must be a case of hatred or
revenue, and so, after due consulta
tion with their chlcf, they started a
?ery careful inquiry among all the
employes and attaches of MacMillan
?* W possible cause or
TIIK* HAD WATCHED HIM PURCHASE FOIR I.ONG COII.S OF POWDER-PAPER .Sl.OW FISE.
tive for such a retaliation. Their ef
forts, however, seemed to meet with
very little success. They could not
find that the llrrn had recently dis
missed any of their native employes,
nor that they had reduced their wages,
nor that they had inflicted any hard
and unjust rules upon them, nor that
any European connected with the es
tablishment hud ill treated or abused
a native?any one of which actions
might have given rise to a feeling of
bitterness or resentment?but they
found that, on the contrary, every
thing' seemed to have been going
along in a most harmonious manner.
Last of all they took up the question
of religion, including various native
customs and prejudices, against
which they knew the European was
apt to protest and stumble and so
start trouble and opposition, but even
liere the firm seemed to have been
entirely innocent. They had not ob
jected to the burning of incense and
the offering of idol money to the
wharf god, under due precautions;
they had not compelled any man to
work upon a native holiday; they had
granted the usual number of absences
for grandparents' funerals and other
ancestral ceremonies and had yielded
very generously to all the little na
tive requests which the compradore
included under the general, all-em
bracing term of "joss pidgin"?in fact,
they had got along together most sat
isfactorily and smoothly. As the se
nior member of the firm expressed it,
"A little oil on the wheels doesn't cost
very much, and it makes all the ma
chinery go smoother."
"Well, Sain," asked the Inspector of
his head Chinese assistant (mistaking
the popular native name of "Life" for
the English abbreviation of Samuel,
which most Europeans do), "what
does your part of the force think put
the fellow or fellows up to It? What
grudge did they have against the
white man this time that led them -to
try to get even with him by setting
his godown on fire?"
"Me no can savee just now," was
tho laconic reply; "s'poseg blme by
can catche. Me tlnkee b'rongee two
tie plecee man have do. Just now
he velly good fllend, no talkee.
S'posee blme by he makee light, one
piece* man come talkee me, one
piecee man go lun away?all time so
fashion."
"Ah! I see; you mean that as long
as the gang- holds together they'll
keep absolute silence, but just as soon
as a row breaks out between them
some of them will turn state's evi
dence and inform the police, Just to
get even with the rest of them, eh?
That's about the way they do things
at home and in other countries."
"Me tinkee so fashion."
"1 guess you're about right, my
friend, as the American consul says,
and about how long is it before they
come to this falling out?"
"No man can talkee Hue, but two
weekee more b'longee big Chinee
feastee, all samee water joss him
catchee birthday, plenty piecee wharf
coolie dlinkee sam shu get dlunkee,
bime by in.'ikee fight."
"Well, we'll wait and see what turns
up after the holiday; in the mean
time keep a sharp eye open for any
evidence from other quarters, see?"
"Me can s'clure," was the assurance
of the faithful Sam.
? ? ? ? ? ? ?
The old Arm of MacMillan & Co. that
had been for so many years a well
known name in Shanghai and the
other ports had recently dissolved,
following the death of the last of the
MacMillan family, and the good will
of the establishment, together with
the firm's name and their Chinese
chop of "Met ang," or "Beautiful
Wave," had been sold out to a Eura
sian relative named Peasley and his
Portuguese friend Itodero. Being nat
urally most anxious to keep up ttye
trade of the old house and to continue
the good will of the Chinese, they had
made very few changes in the native
staff, and so, as far as the general
public was concerned, the firm of
"Mel L.ang" was still doing business
at the old stand by the Australian
wharf, where they had been for so
many years and where every Jin rlk
eha puller and cargo coolie knew the
address.
Messrs. Peasely and Rodero decided
to offer the customary reward for the
arrest of the lncendtary-v-not from
any very high motive, but largely to
keep themselves in good standing at
the Merchants' Club and with the for
eign community generally?u|
they inserted the following advertise
ment in the local papers, both foreign
and native:
Reward!
The sum of 500 Mexican dollars
will be paid by us for information
that will lead to the arrest and
conviction of the party or parties
who set Are to our river godown
on the night of July 17.
MacMILLAN & CO,
(Chinese chop "Mei Lang")
Whang Poo road.
In less than three days after this
had circulated freely all over the set
tlement and was being discussed in
every teashop from the customhouse
to the international boundary, two na
tive visitors sought out the person
of Sam, the detective, and put In a
claim for share of the reward by
giving him what they considered a
very important clue. He listened care
fully to all that they had to say and
then reported it to his superior officer,
Capt. Brownlow. The testimony was
to this effect, viz: They had seen
Nam Tuck, the night watchman em
ployed at the Australian wharf, just
to the north of MacMillan's, enter a
native shop on the Whang Poo road
on the evening of July 16?the night
before the Are?and had watched him
purchase four long coils of powder
paper slow fuse, such as are used by
natives during the long summer nights
for lighting their pipes. To be sure,
he might have been perfectly innocent
in the purchase, but the date and the
hour and the exact number of the
fuses looked very suspicious, and bo
the web of circumstantial evidence
began gradually to weave itself about
him and to point to him as having
concealed the rolls of slow-buixiing
paper In the four different corners of
the godown on the day of the confla
gration. Brownlow reported the mat
ter to his chief, wltlj the result that It
was decided to place the unsuspecting
Nam Tuck forthwith under arrest,
and to put hltn through an official
grilling before he had any chance to
confer with his friends or cronies. The
native officers found him at his home
early the following morning and he
was securely locked In a cell at head
quarters before breakfast.
When he was brought up for the
preliminary Investigation Inspector
Qubblns thought It would be well for
the British vice consul to attend and
to bring hla official Interpreter with
him. (For although the consul was
a proficient Chinese scholar, he spoke
only the northern mandarin tongue of
Peking and would have great difficulty
In understanding the local dialect of
Nlng Fo or Shanghai.) The interpre
ter being away on a vacation, the con
sul brought In hla place Archdeacon
Wiggins of the English Church mis
sion, who not only apoke the dialects
like g native, but who. from hla long
4UU0AJC Um pcwl* 1?U
mate knowledge of their ways, was
always of Invaluable assistance in un
raveling these international tangles.
It was a cause of intense relief and
gratification to Nam Tuck?to Bay
nothing of his astonishment?to find
himself in the presence of a foreigner
familiar enough with his own tongue
to converse with him directly, and so,
after a little hesitation, he told the
archdeacon his story and the latter
In turn repeated it in English to the
foreign officers.
He acknowledged the purchase of
the fuses on the afternoon of July 16,
remembering the exact date by the
fact that on that day he had drawn
three strings of brass cash out of the
"well" (dry hole in the ground where
the Chinese conceal their money), and
also acknowledged the exact number
of four. He explained, however, that
he Intended them only for the purpose
for which lie always used them, that
was to light his pipe during the night
and to keep away the mosquitoes.
"Please ask him how he came to buy
exactly four?no more, no legs," said
Inspector Gubbins. turning to the
archdeacon; "that's not the usual na
tive way of selling goods, is it? I
thought they always sold them In
packages of ten. corresponding to our
dozen in England."
After a lot of chattering back and
forth between the missionary and the
prisoner?which seemed to Brownlow
long enough to have asked the ques
tion forty times over and to have an
swered it In forty different ways?the
missionary said: "He bought just four
because that happened to be all the
cash that he had brought with him in
his girdle. He says the shopkeeper,
who knows him well, will gladly tes
tify thut he buys them there regu
larly and. either singly or in pack
ages of ten as a rule, but sometimes
in odd amounts if he doesn't happen
to. have, the cash. He denies all
knowledge of the origin of the- Are :
and swears that the very first he
knew about it was when he saw
smoke and flames bursting out under
the roof on the northern side and ran
out Into the street to summon the
foreign policeman."
Further questioning on the part of
Hie officers and the visitors failed to
bring-out anything further from the
watchman or to shake in any way his
direct and simple testimony. It was
thought best, however, not to release
him at once, so he was remanded to
his cell for further safekeeping and
the consul returned with the archdea
con to the office.
?????#?
As soon as it became known In
Heavenly Moon Alley that Nam Tuck
had been arrested and taken away by
the police, the entire population of
that row of native buildings gave
way to screams of excitement and
crowded around Ills family's doorway
to learn the why and the wherefor.
His wife and mother were discovered
rolling on the floor and tearing their
hair, while the children were alter
nately weeping and yelling and run
ning about in every direction. The
Venerable Choo, the "Old Father of
the Alley," as he was affectionately
called by its denizens, immediately
took charge of the situation and be
gan to quiet things down. When order
had been partially restored he eat
himself down in the high seat In the
room and after a sipping of the tea
and a whiff or two of the pipe which
a kind neighbor had provided he
gave It as his counsel that ?"commit
tee from the alley, consisting of the
mother, the eldest son and two re
spectable residents, should proceed
without delay to the head office
of the Australian wliarf and there
protest against Nam Tuck's arrest
and demand his release. Ills age and
position secured immediate compli
ance with his advice and the "com
mittee" waited In due order upon Mr.
Bellsice, the head manager of the
Australian wharf at his office. He
listened very respectfully to their
story and complaint and, assuring
them of his complete confidence in
Nam Tuck and of his intention to do
everything in lils power to clear him
of suspicion, he handed them over to
the compradore for tea and refresh
ments and, calling his own jln-rik
sha, ordered the coolie to take him to
police headquarters.
"What's the meaning of all this,
Gubbins?" he began, as he was ush
ered into the private office. "Why
have you pounced upon my night
watchman and locked him up without
a bit of evidence? He hadn't any mora
to do with that MacMillan fire than I
had?not a bit, and you fellows kn?w
it, too. But I suppose, as usual, you
had to make a scapegoat of somebody
and so you listened to the first fel

POISONED HER HUSDAND
%
A Jury at Bozeman Found Mrs* ,l%
Guilty of Manslaughter*
WAS OUT FOR THIRTEEN HOURS
The Fair Defendant Shows Little Concern and Smiles and
Chats While Awaiting the Verdict—End of a
Famous Case.
Bozeman, Mont., Feb. 13.—At a lit
tle before 10 o'clock this morning the
jury in the Black murder case
brought in a verdict of manslaughter
against Mrs. Lucy S. Black. The
jury was out about thirteen hours.
Judge Holloway will sentence the
prisoner Saturday morning.
The verdict was no surprise to
many people, as nearly everybody ap
peared to think the jury would either
acquit or find the defendant guilty of
manslaughter.
CHIEF AND MAGISTRATE FIGHT.
Sensation Sprung in Helena Police
Circles.
Helena, Feb. 14.—The latest sensa
tion in Helena is the friction between
the chief of police and the police
magistrate. Chief of Police Travis
ordered Police Magistrate Walker
out of his office and told him never to
cross the door again. The chief in
a card in a. local paper said he will
prepare charges against the magis
trate at the meeting of the city coun
cil next Mondav. He does not say
what the charges are, but throws out
hints and inuendos regarding the
magistrate.
TO AID HAVRE.
Immigration to be Diverted to the
• Milk River Valley.
Butte, Feb. 15.—It is announced to
be the intention of the Great North
ern to divert as much immigration
into the Milk River valley as possi
ble this spring. The territory is
tributary to Havre, which city ex
pects to receive much benefit from
the movement. It is anicipated that
no less than 5,000 settlers will be in
duced to take up lands within a ra
dius of fifty miles of that city.
ROSS HAS RESIGNED.
He Confirms the Statement That He
Will Go With fhe Missouri Pacific.
Great Falls, Feb. 15.—Superintend
ent Ross of the Montana Central rail
way passed through the city this aft
ernoon on the west bou'ffd train. He
was here but a few minutes, but con
firmed the report that he had re
signed from the Montana Central to
accept a position with the Missouri
Pacific at St. Louis.
INTERNATIONAL ASSOCIATION.
The Women's Suffrage Association
Will Embrace the World.
Washington, Feb. 14.—In the con
vention of the National Woman's
Suffrage association, held here today,
several notable papers were read,
and -among other business trans
acted it was agreed to organize a
permanent International Woman's
Suffrage conference, and Susan B.
Anthony was elected chairpian. The
representation will be extended to
all countries. New Orleans was se
lected as the next place to hold the
convention.
THEY FOUGHT A DRAW.
"Silent" Rowan and Philip Green
Afraid of Each Other.
Salt Lake,' Feb. 14.—One of the
largest crowds ever gathered around
a Salt, Lake City ring side wit
nessed "Silent" Rowan of Anaconda
and Philip Green of Butte fight a 20
round draw here tonight. Taking it
all around,* the contest was very
tame, both fighters apparently being
afraid of each other.
WILL SUCCEED ROSS.
E. L. Brown of Duluth to Be Super
intendent of Montana Central
Duluth, Minn., Feb. 14.—Edward L.
Brown, superintendet of the Lake
Superior divisio of the Northern Pa
cific railroad and vice president of
the Union Depot and Transfer com
pany, yesterday tendered his resig
tion to accept an offer to become
general superintendent of the Mon
tana Central railroad Mr. Brown de
clined- to be interviewed.
While the jury was waiting for
Judge Holloway to arrive, the defend
ant talked pleasantly and smiled as
though everything was as it should
be, and when the verdict was read,
was one of the coolest inmates of the
court house.
ft is probable that her attorneys
will move for a new trial Saturday.
The trial consumed the greater part
of nine days and has been one of the
most sensational in the history of
the county, or even the state.
NO WHITE LABOR.
Be
Iwenty-one Patrol Boats Wi
Built by Chinese Labor.
Over $750,000 in contracts for 21
steamers for use as coast patrol boats
and revenue cutters in Philippine wa
ters have been placed through the
civil commission at Manila with En
glish firms in Japan and China.
Coolie labor will be employed in the
construction of these vessels, and
great indignation is expressed at the
ship building plants and' navy yards
of the coasv at the action of the gov
ernment in placing the contracts
with foreign firms. Even the Cavite
navy yard, near Manila, could have
built the craft, but the commission
has seen fit to get the work done in
China, Japan and India, because it
can be done cheaper there.
The dispatch states that bids were
opened in December and contracts
have since been placed. Under these
the steamers will be built by seven
or eight shipbuilding firms of Japan,
Snanghai, Hongkong and Singapore.
Twenty steamers will each be 160
feet long, and one, to be used as the
flagship of the squadron, will be
about 200 feet long.
The matter of building these
steamers by coolie labor in Asiatic
ports has apparently been kept in the
background for fear of arousing op
position in this country. The object,
of course, is to save time and ex
pense by having them constructed as
near Manila as possible.
Specifications were prepared dur
ing the late fall under the direction
of Commander Marix, captain of the
port of Manila, who later took them
before the shipbuilding concerns of
Oriental cities. Most of these firms
had representatives present when the
bids were opened. The total cost
will approximate $700,000.— From
San Francisco Bulletin.
The navy department is now mak
ing arrangements and are asking
congress to appropriate thirty-two
million dollars for a navy yard at
Subig bay, Philippine islands.
Herein lies the greatest menace to
American labor of modern times. The
ships of the American navy built by
the Chinese coolie labor at Hong
kong and the ships of the navy re
peired r/, Subig bay by Chinese
coolie mechanics.
The scale of wages paid out there
is for all classes of mechanical labor
about 50 cents in gold per day. The
same class of work pays from $3.50
to $4 in America.
If they build and repair the ships
by Chinese coolies, what will the
American mechanic do who pays the
taxes to support this government?
They will soon have strikes and
starvation staring them in the face.
—Valejo Chronicle.
ESCAPED FROM JAIL.
Lest Slater Walks Out of the Forsyth
Basti le.
Forsythe, Feb. 14.—Lest Slater,
who has been confined in the county
jail for the past week, charged with
robbery, broke out tonight about 7
o'clock and, though the officers were
on his track shortly afterward, he
has not been found. Slater secured
a bar and worked the bolts out of the
door and walked out, no one being
near to molest him. Slater was ar
rested about a week ago for entering
tbe house of E. A. Torell on Tongue
river and stealing a revolver and
other articles.

CAPTAIN RICHMOND P. HOBSON.
Captain Richmond P. Hobson, he of Merrimac fame, will be seen neither
on the lecture platform nor in politics. He recently made application for
retirement from the navy on the grounds of poor eyesight, but the retiring
board rejected his application, and the gallant captain must either remain in
the service or give up his pay as a naval officer. It is said that he hoped to
••nter politics and give lectures.
E DEAD
ONE WOUNDED
Fearful Results of the Ken
tucky Moonshine Fight
TO BE REINFORCED
Lee Turner, Owner of the "Burned House"
Scouring the Hills for Aid. An
other Battle Expected.
Middleboro, Ky., Feb. 14.—Accord
ing to the latest report fromthe scene
of yesterday's fight at Lee Turner's
nine men were killed and one mor
tally wounded. Deputy Sheriff Charles
Cecil and seven mountaineers are
dead. John Boyle of the sheriff's
posse, who was mortally woünded,
died today.
Theré were over sixty men engaged
in the fight.
It was reported here on the streets
tonight that Lee Turner, proprieor of
the burned "Quarter House," who es
caped from the palisade with his
brother, "General", during the battle
yesterday afternoon has been all day
scouring the mountains for the pur
nose of gathering around him men
enough to come against the Middle
boro officers who burned his fort and
killed his stanchest supporters. The
Middleboro men are greatly alarmed
and have ' mustered into service all
who are available. The band that
went after Turner yesterday have re
mained together all day. Last night
ten coal miners from Excelsior went
to the Mingo mines where Turner is
staying and offered him their serv
ices. Mrs. Turner, Lee's wife, was
seen tonight by an Associated Press
correspondent and asked for her
opinion on the situation. She said
that she had not heard from her hus
band since the battle occurred except
once, when 'he telephoned her that
be was safe, but is of the opinion
that Turner will remain in the moun
tains and rebuild his place. She a lo o
said that Turner was a man who did
not know what defeat is and if it
were necessary he would put a stand
ing army of 500 mountaineers in hi?
fort.
TEDDY, JR., IS BETTER.
In View of His Condition Roosevelt
Has Returned to Washington.
Groton, Mass., Feb. 14.—Theodore
Roosevelt, Jr., has improved very
rapidly and for the first time since
his illness he partook of solid food.
Meat being included in tbe diet. The
boy rested comfortable all day. The
president has returned to Washing
ton.
IS TOLSTOI DEAD.
Vienna Newspaper Publishes Rumor
to That Effect.
Vienna,Feb. 15.—Tfie Neusteweiner
Journal published an unconfirmed re
port that Count Tolstoi is dead.
FOR DEEDS
OF BRAVERY
Medals are Awarded to Heroes
of the Late Campaigns.
TITUS ON THE LIST
The Musician Who First Scaled the Wal's
of Pekin Will be Given a
Medal.
Washington, Feb. 14.—Adjutant
General Corbin today made public
the report of the army board of
which General MacArthur was presi
dent, appointed some time ago to con
sider and report the names of offi
cers and enlisted men of the army
who distinguished themselves during
the recent campaigns in Cuba, China
and the Philippines, so as to entitle
them to the award either of medals
of honor or certificates of merit.
With the exception that Col. Theo
dore Roosevelt he given the brevet
rank of brigadier general for mer
itorious services at the battle of San
Juan hill, they have been recom
mended by Preshlent Roosevelt. Mu
sician Calvin P. Titus, who was the
first, to scale the walls at Pekin and
who afterward was sent to West
t'oint, and Lieutenant Matthew A.
Batson, who commanded the Maca
bebe scouts, are among those men
tioned in the list. The certificate of
merit are all awarded to non-com
missioned officers and privates. Of
tbe total number of 56, four are on
account of the Cuban campaign, 45
on account of the Philippine cam
paign and - seven on account of the
Chinese trouble.
CHINESE MUST GO.
Residents of a Colorado Town Drive
Out Celestials.
Denver, Col., Feb. 14.—Governor
Orman today made public the fol
lowing telegram, which he received
last Monday from Secretary Hay:
"Washington, Feb. 9.—His Excel
lency, James B. Orman, Governor,
Denver, Col.: It is represented that
the Chines 3 residents, about 70 in
number, have been ordered to leave
Silverton, under threats of violence.
An appeal has been made through
the Chinese minister for their pro
tection.
The department would be pleased
if you would take such action as
you may find necessary for the pro
tection of their rights of person, busi
ness and property.—John Hay, Sec
retary of State."
Governor Orman said today that
he knew nothing of any trouble at
Silverton until ne received Secretary
Hay's telegram, but he immediately
wrote the sheriff of San Juan county
to send him full particulars of the
trouble and to see that no violence
is done the Chinese there. Until he
hears from the sheriff he can take
no steps in the matter, further than
A HOT ROAST
FROM WHEELER
The Kentucky Congressman Scores the
President and Secretary of State*
CALLS HAY A PITIABLE FLUNKY
The Speech Aroused Much Interest and Created a Decided
Sensation In the House—The Statesman Also
Took a Shot at Prince Henry.
Washington, Feb. 14.—The house
was enlivened today by a very sen
sational speech by C. K. Wheeler of
Kentucky, in denuciation of what he
deemed "flunkeyism" to foreign
countries. He took recent statements
emanating from continental cabinets
regarding the attitude of Great Brit
ain during the Spanish war as a text
for a wholesale attack upon the
trend of American diplomacy. His
speech aroused the house to a high
pitch of excitement and was chiefly
directed against the government of
Great Britain. In speaking of Lord
Pauncefote, the British ambassador,
he said: "If he comes here to rep
resent his government, well and
good; but if he comes here to hatch
conspiracies, to attempt to force his
wishes and his ideas upon the Amer

A special to thé Post from Silver
ton today says: Of the fifty Chinese
who resided in Silverton ten days
ago, twenty-five remain. The re
maining twenty-five, it is believed,
will depart within the next week or
so. When the last Chinaman leaves
the ban against the yellow man will
be strictly enforced, and hereafter no
Mongolians will be allowed in this
district.
The hegira of the past ten days is
due to the boycott declared two
weeks ago by the union laboring men
of Silverton, including the Miners'
union, the Federal Labor union and
the Cooks and Walters' union. These
unions declared against patronizing
either Chinese restaurants or laun
dries, and their proprietors were
quietly informed that they could not
remain here.
The Chinese are not being hurried
in their departure, but it is generally
understood that they must leave this
community within a reasonable time.
Before the boycott was ordered there
were from six to eight Chinese res
taurants and about a dozen laun
dries. Now there are only two res
taurants conducted by Chinese and
the number of laundries has been re
I duced materially. No violence has
been attempted against the Chinese
i so far.

HAS NO KICK COMING.
Russia Receives News of England
Japan Treaty With Pleasure.
St. Petrsburg, Feb. 14.—-The Rus
sian official view of the Japanese
treaty was communicated to the cor
respondent of the Associated Press
as follows: "We accept the news of
the entente with complete equanim
ity and are happy to learn that Eng
land and Japan are pledged to main
tain tbe integrity of China and the
independence of Corea, two princi
ples which Russia was the first to
establish as the basis of her foreign
policy in the Orient."
ATTEMPTED SUICIDE.
Big Timber, Mont., Feb. 14.—Word
was received here yesterday of the
attempted suicide of Miss Bessie
Boyce, a young lady residing at Big
Elk. She took carbolic acid. When
discovered the young lady was suffer
1 ing great agony and it was only by
I the timely administration of emetics
: that her life was saved. It is said
: that Miss Boyce attempted this rash
^ act upon her life because of jeal

\yasnington, Feb. 14.—Pensions
granted: Robert Blakely Albright,

then paid his respects to Secretary

TRIAL FOR ASSAULT.
Negro at Fort Benton Accused of a
Dastardly Crime.
Fort Benton, Feb. 14.—The trial of
the negro, Johnson, charged with as
sault on Mrs. Charles Rose, at Havre,
occupied the time of the district
court today. The testimony was to
the effect that on February 2, the ac
cused prevailed on Charles Rose to
take a walk, leaving Mrs. Rose
asleep. In the Havre beer hall John
son got Rose to drink a glass of beer.

Shanghai. April 1 W) War has
warped the land and great things
have come upon China but the
coolie, when he is not impressed into
one army or another, often to be
shot as he plods under his heavy
load, goes on today as he did when '
th Manchus ruled this vast domain, j
; For him life has only one mran
j ing It is labor. So he chants as i
I he trudges along to chase away the ;
I devils of fatigue. .
j The coolie is the one thing in
i China that has remained unchanged.
Politics has caught the fancy of
students and professional classes,
and, to som,; extent, that of the
business man. The older anil more
conservative Chinese accept the new .
ord'-r with -the best grace they can. j
Most of the reluctant believe it is
useless to hold out longer and are
Europeanizing or Americanizing ,
themselves. But the coolie foes on
as be has since the year were
young, eating his rice and fish, when
he can afford fish, and sleepinc pre.
sumably a dreamless sleep under
some makeshift shelter.
The newcomer is suspicious of this
chanting at first, but he soon learns
it is not theatrical nor an Indica
tion of a Joyous heart but is the out
ward manifestation of an age-old
belief that In this way the spirits
that tire the body can be driven out
The coolis wears the garb of cen
turies ago, the butter bowl hat,
cheap wooden shoes or slippers if
anything on his feet and around his
equator there I Its tfcaa tb law
would allow In other eoantxiaa.

Heavens! what au escape 1 'Tis the third
scorpion I have seen today, and, dropping
down upon my very hand, this time ! Ugh 1
did it sting me ? I shiver to my very heart, I
cannot think, for horror! Their venom, too, is
intense, they say. Scarce six hours between
the bite and death ! And such a death 1 Livid,
-wollen, racked as if hot lead were flowing in
c-u?*'s veins, aud the mad wildness of fierce delirium
I * * * This loathsome den grows
more and more insupportable. Its cracks and
corners full of hideous reptiles, lizards sporting
over me as 1 sleep, and the damp, slimy walls
and floor reeking with foul exudations, the very
smell of which is sickening. What wonder I
aui ill ? I' bret la its own malaria, such a den
as this. Thank God, mv work is nearly over,
and then I shall leave here. Too late, perhaps.
Aye, this dampness, this mephitic air,
has done its work already. My bones are full
of creeping chills?my head throbs?my face is
ilushed?my pulse jerks and dances, madly,
under my linger. Fated! 1 know what this
means?typhus?delirium?death I Just, too,
when I am becoming reconciled to life?when
1 have a prospect ahead?a chance of redemption
and security 1 Ob, was ever more accursed
lot thau mine 1 What is it ? * * * I felt
no pain, I see no mark. * * * Could that
reptile Lave stung me ? This is very strange 1
I have never known sensations before like these.
What can it be? My limbs are numb and
chill?I can scarce move my feet?scarce lift
inv hand to my brow. IIow it throbs, and burns,
and swells, as if it were one single great fevered
pulse, into which a thousand hearts were forcing
their serpent-poisoned blood ! What is this
tightness in my chest ? this pressure of ten
thousand pounds upon me ?* Am I dying? If
I but close my eyes a second, how my head
reels, like a staggering ship, that, squall-struck,
j)?i isra, nunuui^f m LICI iukuucu^U tuuiae.
" * * What can it mean T * * * Ah,
fool! triple, insensate fool, so dreading death,
yd sealing fate with your own rash hand 1 What
means it, indeed! Poisoned?killed?self-slain?
involuntary suicide! Why take the drug so
rashly, doubling the dose? Ha! thou black
AT'-nrsed fiend! dost thou grin at me, and
mock me! thou, deeming thyself safe within
thy bottle-prison? Hell burn thee! Thus I
dash thee now against the wall; none other
shall thou kill 1 * * * Death! is that its
meaning? So soon ! Oh, Father of Mercies.
I thought to make my peace?I?I have wished
well for men! * * * I have done some
things well ; * * * and Thou?Thou art
Judge of * * * my purposes, * * * and
they .-ay Hull is paved with many, fully as good
as mine. * * * Were it not * * * too
late. * * *
And so, drowned in the stupefying fumes of
the foul drug, numb with instant fear of Death?
t<> Sleep! A'retched expedient of a panicstricken
wretch, was Sleep, for him !
W 3hall not dwell upon his Dreams. In
sleep, they came before him, and peered into1
his face, grotesque, distorted images of himself.
Sleep was no longer to him *' Death's halfbrother,"
who, with drowsy wand, bids all the
world to rest. For him, instead of " knitting
up the ravelled sleeve of care," it rehearsed the
nightly task of Penelope, unweaving what had
been woven by day. It was, in effect, simply
his introduction to another world, a world more J
active than the world he waked in, a world !
which he entered by no "ivory pate," but,
through a dismal dungeon-door, over which
wsis written: " Through me is the way into
eternal pain ; through me the way among the
people lost; "f a world where Chaos was the
order, and "Tohu-Boliu" Sovereign and Pontiff.
Thither the foul Homuneulus of the bottle
was wont to lead him. to drag him on, and,
flinging him iuto the midst ol' the spectres who
peopled this Nifelheim, this "Cloud-Goblin
Laud,"' delighted to see tbeni make sport of
him.
Very soon after he had begun to pnt himself
in the power of Opium, his dreams had taken
the general form of Nightmares. Kither he
was on the point of achieving limitless fortune,
but for some-misadventure; or, there was a
paper mislaid, upon which depended countless
millions; or, the ship which was coming into
port with store of gold for him. sank before his
eyes ; or, there was au error in his calculations,
which he could not correct; or, he saw the robb
rs spot! bim, without power of outcry, or of
p'.rsuit ; or, the slaves ran away with his swift*
ships ; or, the Telegraph gave false rumors ; or,
the nionev proved to be counterfeit, when rei
: or, the horse, that was about to win for
bun, fell, and broke his neck, or some such phanUsraa
infested perpetually his vision, as goon as
he closed his eyes in sleep.
By and by, the dreams assumed another and
uore painful character. For instance, he was
in Wall street, with all the burden of his shame
nnd disgrace upon his shoulders, and no way of
escape. He ran hither and thither, he climl>ed
' :i?r llights of stairs, plunged down into gloomy
cellars, but?all was Wall street, everywhere he
went was Wall street?Wall street, with all
tliOie familiar faces sueeriug, all those myriad
fitters pointed at him, even the fingers of the
din new?-boys ; and no chance of escape for
him! Trinity steeple still pursued him with
-badow, and its chimes still rang in his ear:
Vi.it nre n ihirf n ihlef. a thief! Vou are a
J J v , ^ #
/' or, lie had his money j it wap made,
ar,; - re d away?but, alas ! he could not use it.
tor, down there, in tho coll, the notes in the
1 -k. and the coins in bags, rustled and clinked
a perpetual chorus, that continually grew louder
and louder: *' Embezzled J^nds! embezzled
Jtth li 'they cried, in soprano, tenor, and
a . j rowing louder each moment. Hush! I
- all he betrayed I My children will hear, and
th.- very people in the street 1 Quick, George,
an anvil! Beat upon the wall, till the sweat
Sing with me, so we drown the cursed
rv! 11 jt ever and ever rises the chorus : u Emb:.!>
d funds ! embezzled funds! " louder each
foment. ()b, agony J * * *
Or. there is some foul mark upon his cheek,
say, that he cannot wash away. The people of
?1-e -trkot see it, and avoid him, crossing over to
the other .side, and following him at a distance,
hut turning their heads whenever looks round.
is a loathsome mark, a brand of infamy,
4 -d. so long as it is there, he cannot, dare not,
r He sees how his family shun hira, and
narks the contest they have to undergo between
atuing and affection. Even Tie is sickened
y it, and bates himself lor it. Eagerly he
Plunges into bath, but it will not out; eagerly
!> uses the razor, till the skin itself is pared
yet the ' damned spot" abides there still.
Ah! how he clutches at it, tears it, digs into

-us complete control of it, and a thousand mes^ugt-fs
Hre ready to bring him his advices,
"arming hot-foot, and bearing the yellow-en ftioped
missives aloft in their hands. Here
' juies one : latest News. Failure of Beale
yyd the forger 1 Ruin everywhere ! Horrible
'(S'dation among widows and orphans ! Pshaw 1
yss lt aside ! Another yellow envelope: Many
'ffisands starring for bread. All crying out

against Btale Lloyd! Another?and another,
. .11 ?-l_ I C.,.? J I

and prostitution, and murder,and cannibalism;
and Beale Lloyd the author of it all! Now, the
messengers and the hateful yellow missives
come pouring in upon him by thousands?
messengers of all ages, and complexions,
and races?poor and rich, in siik and vel
vet, in rags and nakedness?missives in all
languages, in every character known on the
face of th# globe?ail telling the same tale of
shame and horror! Shut the door?drive the
bolt?yet here, they come again by the window!
Put hands before your eyes! oh, God t 'tis
written there too, in letters of blood: Robber
of widows and orphans I Starvation, and crime,
and prostitution, and murder, and cannibal
ism?arffl Beale Lloyd the author of it all!
There was to be a race, he dreamt, and he
had ventured largely upon the resuli. A vast
throng, loud talking, pushing, eager, were gathered
about the course, waiting the event. There
sat the judges, mounted aloft in their stand;
there, with particolored show, sparkle of jewels,
tiuttf r of ribbon, rustle of silk, a throng of lovely
women, too; while all the ground was packed
with men. But, where are the horses who
are to run ? Is it a race, when the course is
thus blooked, hurdle-fenced, and ditehed ? Why,
no; it must be steeplechase?but, where are
the red-coats? Hush! St! St! Hark! The
judges rise. Silence in front, there?we shall
hear the entries. " Heme Lloyd, betrayer oj
trust, robber of widows' and orphans, will run
a race wtih Shame! Cruel! * * * ell
eyes bent on him, * * * a million eyes, at
least! * * * on many brows a scowl, * * *
on every li)? a sneer! Away from here 1 How
the hot tlush of shame mantles to his cheek
and brow! Out of the shoving, surging crowd!
No?there is no escape I See, they stand apart
from him?all those million eyes gazing, * * *
all those dark scowling brows, * * * all
those sneer-wreathed lips 1 * * * There
is no escape. He is at bay. * * * Aha!
Yes! To the course, and away?away from
here, forever! 'Tis but a short flight, and
then?no more those scowls, those sneers, those
million eyes! (Courage! Be swift, my good
limbs! Bent nead, clenched teeth, elbows
drawn back?on ! on! We reach it now?over
the barrier?quick ! Fly, dog, for thy life!
FK-! Ha! ha! ha! Tally ho! The game a
afoot! And laughter long, loud, jeering, inextinguishable,
as the laughter of Olympian
gods, when Hephaistos superseded Hebe, bursts
from the crowd as they break ranks and rush
along, with shouts, at his very heels ! No need
to spur nor whip. He runs well who runs for
life. * * * Ha! what was that shout ? A
mad man striking right and left among the
crowd?wild, furious?doing his battles. And !
now?they have him down?'tis Rupert! * * *1
but?no time to pause, now! On! on! on!|
Tally ho! Talty ho! and away! The thief ,
runs well! Hot breath of the shouting, jeer- '
ing mob, warm upon his back?and their inex- |
tinL'uishable lau"liter in his cars. But on. on!
'Tis better so, than to face those million eyes, \
those scowls, those lips, all sueer and scoff! j
On?past the grand stand, now. How the women
natter their fans, and smile, and tinkle the
sweet music of their laughter ! See! one rises,
horror in her face. She outstretches her arms.
She yearns towards him, hunted as he is. She j
cries "Father! Father!" and, with a wild,
piercing shriek, topples over in a swoon!
Helen! * * *
The low, black-hulled slaver steals noiseless
out from the sweltering, torpid stream, and
cleaves the leaden waters with her sharp, 6wift
prow. He sits upon her deck. She is his?
her cargo his?and, once in Cuba, will enrich
him. Around, the savage crew, black browed
wretches, savages, cut throats, pirates. Note
the obstvne jest, the harsh, raucous laugh, the
filthy objurgation, the impious blasphemy of
oath. " fhe owner? " say they, " what care we
for his presence? He is one of us?and as
bad." One of them 1 Oh the shame of the
thought, to him, sitting there under the awning,
upon the sun-blistered deck ! One of them 1
A fellow to those boarded thieves?comrade,
help meet of those swarthy, black browed savages
and pirates! * * * Now hatches are
uul&shed, aud from the steaming hold, blending
with the intolerable stench and foetor of it,
comes a strange murmur of feeble voices, chattering
unknown tongues. Now come up, rapidly,
clumsily, a mass of black forms, naked
and unseemly, and down upon the deck they
squat, listless, with eyes that stare inanely, and
lins_that hang prone and maudlin. What could
rfltte them from their stunned inanition, save
t^Right of the queen jungle of mangroves,
and the rustle of home breezes 'mongst their
native palms ? * * * There they squat,
shivering, vet moving not, when the water is
dashed upon them?whining, yet moving not,
when the heavy-booted crew go in amongst
them, and kick, and cuff them, * * * there
they squat, listless, inane, staring, * * *
staring at him ! Why do their dull, obese, unwinking
eyes ever stare thus at him, watching
him, following him everywhither he moves ?
The gigantic chieftains, braves in their own
land, refractory captives here, sullenly beud
upon him eyes of fierce disdain. Children,
gaunt and terror-stunned, stare at him, wideeyed,
as lord of their destinies?would, at his
bidding, prostrate themselves, and worship him,
the awful Oboe-man, whose power has plucked
them from their homes, add prisons them in
that black, filthy, sickening deu. Mothers, with
infants at their breasts, hush their importunate
cries, and whisper, in fright: "Hush! yonder's
the Oboe-man?he will wring your ne$k!"
" Panama'* ruants shall long look pile,
When Rising hum*inspires the tale;
Chili's dark matr. n* long >hall tame
Tee froward child with Certiam's name."
Aye, and he is Bertram?or worse. There is
some heroism iu the Buccaneer, but where's the
respect that can be claimed for the kidnapper,
the vile wretch who, tor sake ot filthy pelf, steals
and enslaves a crew of besotted, iuane, defenceless
negroes? Still staring at him, mowing,
gibbering. Damnation ! It will drive him mad!
Howl, curse, resist, turn upon your oppressors,
fight, slay us, or die fighting?anything, but
these looks?these fixed, questiouing, unwinking
stares I At night, they hannt him in bis
berth. If he looks aloft, a " booby," perched
on yard-arm, repeats those horrid stares. If
he goes to the rail, and gazes at the bubbling
wake, lo! a white shark, stealing noiseless after,
speculates upon him with its glassy, cruel eye,
and claims him for its own. No escape?no
freedom from the questioning, the shame, the
rebukes, the horrors, of those eyes J * * *
And the days move on, and the nights, and,
with their progress, ever a deeper distillation of
horror mingles itself into the substance of his
visions. From the circle of Shame, the batwinged
fiend of Opium bore him downward,
ever downward, and now he was centred within
the black circle of Terror, haunted by every serpent-form
and every nervous thought that could
shock with wild dismay?

And bo it was with his dreams : Nor Libya, hot
and sandy, though it breedeth serpents as the
carrion breeds maggots, nor Ethiopia, nor Red
Sea lauds, with all their wealth of foulest reptiles;
Chelydri, foetid as a dragon's breath;
far-springing Jaculi; Ceochris, stiff and speckled,
as if with their victim's blood, double-headed,
bivions " Amphisboenas dire; " and cepperhued
Pareas, the hideously-fatal Cobra de Capello,
with its erect aspect and hooded front?
none of these lands, though thus infested,
" showed ever plagues so numerous or so dire "
as these which now transfixed him in his sleep.
It was one incessant rule of horror, from
which he would start up,"Screaming, to weep,
through very weakness, and the strong revulsion
of his bouI, when he felt George's kindly
hand touch soothingly his sweating brow. And
how grateful he was to this ever-watching friend
for waking him:
" Hia life bad crept M long on a broken wing
Tbrj' cells o I madness, hannu of horror a no fear,
Tnat h= came tu b; grateful at last for a lit le thing."
Alas for that murderous sleep! All his hopes
retorted themselves on him, coming writhing
back to him, like serpents, and stinging him the
^
IE N
WASH
fiercer, because he had nursed them, on a time, <
in his bosom. His back-broken pride was long
since dead to dream, but his humility sufficed
not, even although it assumed the guise of shivering,
chill-struck, blighted terror, crouching
like the lap-wing to the very dirt. 6uch hideous
dreams! He dreamt of coin, which, when he
touched it, clnng to his fingers with the tenacity
of burning phosphorus, eating in to the bone,
with the heat of the very burning lake that
burneth with fire and brimstone. His treasurefreighted
ships, himself on board, now sunk,
mastless, storm-opprest, now the spoil of fire,
floated off helmless, after the shrieking crews
had ceased to know more pain. So the dreams
rushed on. bearing him ever nearer Hell.
He was in his cell, he dreamt, and, weary
with long work, and strain of heavy thought, he
had lain down to try and rest for awhile, until
the leaden obstruction should pass away, and
free his limbs once more. The smoky lamp
waned slowly, and its feeble flicker lent a spectra^
character to the long shadows of the room.
Ah, this damned gnawing at my vitals! thought
he. The rats?how they gnaw me?how they
listen at the cricks and crevices of my broken
heart 1 Arsenic, now, or strychnine?and, I am
free. So?so?they have almost eaten their
way out, though. * * * I wonder?'tis
strange, now, such queer shadows I Shadows,
I said ? Perhaps, * * * 'tis passing strange,
surely! Over there, for instanea. that hlaplr I
* * * shadow ? * * * lying, closeprest,
upon the floor, just by the rusty ringj
bolt, * * * where Giovanni Rocco was
chained, perhaps. The flags are worn into hollows,
just there?he must have been able to
i take only three steps in his chafed walk?three
forward, and three back again?then repeat.
That black thing there. * * * What ho 1
| Man, ghost, devil?why prostrate thus? Thou
canst not rival me in woe?equal then in
endurance. Come forth ! He is very tall?for
all his hollow chest and stooping, shoulders.
What a strange pale face it is?livid, I should
call it?the more so for that unearthly black
robe. * * * Coming this way, too, with
shuffling, dubious, irregular steps. Does it
smile, or frown ? The light of those eyes is the
